text: The surgeon mobilizes the left lobe of the liver and dissects the liver hilum, identifying a large hilar cyst and dilated distal bile duct in the gallbladder. The common bile duct is isolated and divided, with cephalic traction applied to the distal bile duct using the third arm. The intrapancreatic portion of the bile duct is dissected down to its union with the pancreatic duct, and the root of the bile duct is clipped and resected, sparing the main pancreatic duct. The left hepatic artery is clamped, causing discoloration of the left lobe, and then resected. The left portal vein is isolated, and vascular control is achieved, including the caudate branch of the portal vein, using wrist-leg movements of the instrument.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy to remove the distended gallbladder, clearing the operative field. The right anterior hepatic duct, draining into the large hilar cyst, is isolated and temporarily ligated using Firefly fluorescent imaging for precise identification. The right posterior hepatic duct remains unisolated due to the risk of vascular injury, with plans to address it during hepatic parenchymal resection.

The surgeon performs parenchymal resection using the rubber band retraction method, suturing both edges of the resection plane with rubber bands fixed through the abdominal wall. The resection plane widens automatically due to the elastic power of the rubber bands. Ultrasonic shears and a bipolar coagulator are used to slowly resect the liver tissue. At the hilar level, the right posterior hepatic duct is safely isolated and resected. The parenchymal transaction is completed by resecting the caudate lobe and left hepatic vein.

The surgeon transects the first portion of the jejunum using an endo-GIA stapler and pulls it through the mesocolon for hepaticojejunostomy. The hepatic ducts are prepared with a short silicon catheter, and ductoplasty is performed to create a common opening. The hepaticojejunostomy is completed with continuous suturing, followed by jejuno-jejunostomy using intracorporeal suturing. The specimen is delivered through a fendant steel skin incision using a plastic bag.
